top_categories.workout_context: At mile 60 of 103, glycogen is gone. This workout teaches your body to produce watts on fumes.
9,843ft of climbing means repeated surges above threshold. VO2max work is how you survive the fifth climb, not just the first.
96ft/mi means long efforts at or near FTP on every climb. If your threshold cracks at minute 15, you'll walk the rest.
Every hard session works better with a bigger aerobic base. At 103 miles, base fitness is the difference between racing and surviving.
At Dreiländergiro, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.
Race-pace efforts that mimic Dreiländergiro's demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before June.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of Dreiländergiro. Train it until it feels boring.
9,843ft of climbing on grades that change every minute. You need to switch from seated to standing without losing rhythm.
Dreiländergiro's mixed terrain forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 103 miles.
103 miles rewards fat oxidation. This volume work shifts your fuel mix so you're still burning fat at hour 5, not bonking at hour 3.
Endurance with intensity spikes baked in. Exactly what 9,843ft of climbing on mixed terrain does to your power file on race day.
Low-cadence force work for 9,843ft of climbing. The slow, grinding ascents where you can't spin—you push.

race_overlay.heat: June in Nauders, Tirol, Austria can bring heat. Complete 3–4 heat exposure sessions in the final 2 weeks before Dreiländergiro. Increase sodium intake 48 hours before race day.
race_overlay.nutrition: Target 60–80g carbs/hour for Dreiländergiro’s 103 miles. Start fueling within the first 30 minutes — early fueling prevents late-race collapse. Bonking at mile 60 is a nutrition failure, not a fitness failure.
race_overlay.altitude: Dreiländergiro with 9,843ft of climbing, much of it above 8,000ft demands altitude preparation. Arrive 5–7 days early for acclimatization. Expect 5–15% power reduction at altitude. Increase iron intake 4 weeks out. Hydrate aggressively — altitude increases fluid loss by 20–40%.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on Durability, VO2max, and TT Threshold to prepare you for 103 miles of mixed terrain in Nauders, Tirol, Austria.
